title: APoW: Auditable Proof-of-Work Against Block Withholding Attacks
summary: We introduce APoW, a novel proof-of-work (PoW) construction inspired by Hashcash-style nonce searching, which enables the auditing of other miners' work through accountable re-scanning of the nonce space. The proposed scheme allows a miner to probabilistically attest to having searched specified regions of the nonce space in earlier mining rounds, while concurrently earning rewards for performing productive work for a new block or pool share. This capability enables miners belonging to a mining pools to audit another miner's claimed effort retroactively, thereby allowing the probabilistic detection of block withholding attacks (BWAs) without requiring trusted hardware or trusted third parties. As a consequence, the construction supports the design of decentralized mining pools in which work attribution is verifiable and withholding incentives are substantially reduced. The scheme preserves the fundamental properties of conventional PoW, including public verifiability and difficulty adjustment, while adding an orthogonal auditability layer tailored to pool-based mining. Finally, while a full deployment of APoW in Bitcoin would require a consensus rule change and minor modifications to mining ASICs, the construction remains practically useful even without consensus changes, for instance, as a pool-level auditing mechanism that enables verifiable pay-for-auditing using existing pool reserves.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces APoW, a new proof-of-work construction that allows for auditing miners' work through nonce space re-scanning, reducing block withholding attacks in decentralized mining pools.
AI.motivation: The motivation for this work is to address the issue of block withholding attacks in mining pools without relying on trusted hardware or third parties, thereby enabling more secure and verifiable decentralized mining pool operations.
AI.method: The method involves the design of APoW, a proof-of-work scheme inspired by Hashcash-style nonce searching that allows miners to probabilistically attest to their prior work and enables retroactive auditing by other pool members.
AI.result: The result of this work is the construction of a proof-of-work system that supports work attribution verification, enabling the probabilistic detection of block withholding attacks and the potential for decentralized mining pools with reduced withholding incentives.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that APoW provides a practical approach to improving the auditability and security of mining pools while maintaining compatibility with the core principles of traditional PoW, such as public verifiability and difficulty adjustment.

title: Coordinated Multi-Domain Deception: A Stackelberg Game Approach
summary: This paper explores coordinated deception strategies by synchronizing defenses across coupled cyber and physical systems to mislead attackers and strengthen defense mechanisms. We introduce a Stackelberg game framework to model the strategic interaction between defenders and attackers, where the defender leverages CVSS-based exploit probabilities and real-world vulnerability data from the National Vulnerability Database (NVD) to guide the deployment of deception. Cyber and physical replicas are used to disrupt attacker reconnaissance and enhance defensive effectiveness. We propose a CVE-based utility function to identify the most critical vulnerabilities and demonstrate that coordinated multilayer deception outperforms single-layer and baseline strategies in improving defender utility across both CVSS versions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Stackelberg game framework and uses coordinated deception strategies with cyber and physical replicas to outperform baseline approaches, improving defender utility using real-world vulnerability data.
AI.motivation: The challenges faced by traditional single-layer or baseline deception strategies in coupled cyber and physical systems motivated the need for coordinated deception approaches that can effectively mislead reconnaissance and improve defense resilience.
AI.method: The paper proposes a Stackelberg game model to represent the strategic attacker-defender interaction. It integrates CVSS-based exploit probabilities and data from the NVD, employing CVE-based utility functions to prioritize vulnerabilities. Cyber and physical replicas are introduced to enhance misdirection effectiveness.
AI.result: Coordinated multilayer deception was shown to outperform single-layer and baseline strategies in terms of defender utility across both CVSS versions, demonstrating improved overall defensive outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating defenses through multilayer deception creates a more resilient strategy in coupled systems, offering higher utility and better tactics against reconnaissance and exploitation efforts by attackers.

title: SWaRL: Safeguard Code Watermarking via Reinforcement Learning
summary: We present SWaRL, a robust and fidelity-preserving watermarking framework designed to protect the intellectual property of code LLM owners by embedding unique and verifiable signatures in the generated output. Existing approaches rely on manually crafted transformation rules to preserve watermarked code functionality or manipulate token-generation probabilities at inference time, which are prone to compilation errors. To address these challenges, SWaRL employs a reinforcement learning-based co-training framework that uses compiler feedback for functional correctness and a jointly trained confidential verifier as a reward signal to maintain watermark detectability. Furthermore, SWaRL employs low-rank adaptation (LoRA) during fine-tuning, allowing the learned watermark information to be transferable across model updates. Extensive experiments show that SWaRL achieves higher watermark detection accuracy compared to prior methods while fully maintaining watermarked code functionality. The LoRA-based signature embedding steers the base model to generate and solve code in a watermark-specific manner without significant computational overhead. Moreover, SWaRL exhibits strong resilience against refactoring and adversarial transformation attacks.

AI.tldr: SWaRL is a watermarking framework for code LLMs that uses reinforcement learning and low-rank adaptation to embed verifiable, compile-correct watermarks, achieving high detection accuracy and resilience against attacks.
AI.motivation: The primary motivation is to protect the intellectual property of code LLM owners by enabling robust, functional watermarking that can survive model updates and resist attacks, overcoming the limitations of manual transformation rules and probability manipulation in existing methods.
AI.method: SWaRL uses a reinforcement learning co-training framework with compiler feedback for functional correctness and a confidential verifier for watermark detectability. It incorporates low-rank adaptation (LoRA) during fine-tuning to transfer watermarking across model versions.
AI.result: SWaRL achieves higher watermark detection accuracy while maintaining code functionality compared to previous methods. It can transfer watermarking information across model updates and resists refactoring and adversarial attacks with minimal computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: SWaRL provides an effective and resilient solution for watermarking code LLMs, overcoming current limitations in accuracy, functionality, and adaptability to model updates and attacks via a reinforcement learning-based and LoRA-integrated approach.

title: LAsset: An LLM-assisted Security Asset Identification Framework for System-on-Chip (SoC) Verification
summary: The growing complexity of modern system-on-chip (SoC) and IP designs is making security assurance difficult day by day. One of the fundamental steps in the pre-silicon security verification of a hardware design is the identification of security assets, as it substantially influences downstream security verification tasks, such as threat modeling, security property generation, and vulnerability detection. Traditionally, assets are determined manually by security experts, requiring significant time and expertise. To address this challenge, we present LAsset, a novel automated framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to identify security assets from both hardware design specifications and register-transfer level (RTL) descriptions. The framework performs structural and semantic analysis to identify intra-module primary and secondary assets and derives inter-module relationships to systematically characterize security dependencies at the design level. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves high classification accuracy, reaching up to 90% recall rate in SoC design, and 93% recall rate in IP designs. This automation in asset identification significantly reduces manual overhead and supports a scalable path forward for secure hardware development.

AI.tldr: LAsset automates hardware security asset identification using LLMs, achieving 90-93% recall while reducing manual effort.
AI.motivation: Manual identification of security assets in complex hardware designs is time-consuming and requires significant security expertise, necessitating an automated approach to improve scalability and efficiency in pre-silicon security verification.
AI.method: LAsset leverages large language models (LLMs) for structural and semantic analysis of hardware design specifications and RTL descriptions. It identifies intra-module security assets and derives inter-module relationships to characterize security dependencies at the design level.
AI.result: LAsset achieves up to 90% recall rate in SoC designs and 93% recall rate in IP designs, demonstrating high classification accuracy for security asset identification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LAsset framework demonstrates effectiveness in automating security asset identification for hardware designs, achieving high recall rates and reducing manual overhead to enable scalable secure hardware development.

title: ProSoftArena: Benchmarking Hierarchical Capabilities of Multimodal Agents in Professional Software Environments
summary: Multimodal agents are making rapid progress on general computer-use tasks, yet existing benchmarks remain largely confined to browsers and basic desktop applications, falling short in professional software workflows that dominate real-world scientific and industrial practice. To close this gap, we introduce ProSoftArena, a benchmark and platform specifically for evaluating multimodal agents in professional software environments. We establish the first capability hierarchy tailored to agent use of professional software and construct a benchmark of 436 realistic work and research tasks spanning 6 disciplines and 13 core professional applications. To ensure reliable and reproducible assessment, we build an executable real-computer environment with an execution-based evaluation framework and uniquely incorporate a human-in-the-loop evaluation paradigm. Extensive experiments show that even the best-performing agent attains only a 24.4\% success rate on L2 tasks and completely fails on L3 multi-software workflow. In-depth analysis further provides valuable insights for addressing current agent limitations and more effective design principles, paving the way to build more capable agents in professional software settings. This project is available at: https://prosoftarena.github.io.

AI.tldr: ProSoftArena introduces a benchmark for evaluating multimodal agents in professional software, exposing their limitations through experiments and offering design insights.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for multimodal agents focus on basic environments, neglecting complex professional software workflows critical in real-world scientific/industrial contexts.
AI.method: The researchers created ProSoftArena, a benchmark with 436 tasks across 13 professional applications, using an execution-based evaluation framework and human-in-the-loop assessment to ensure reliability.
AI.result: Top agents achieved only 24.4% success on L2 tasks and failed L3 workflows, with analysis revealing limitations in multi-software coordination and task complexity.
AI.conclusion: The study lays the groundwork for improving agent capabilities in professional software by identifying current limitations and suggesting design principles.

title: Adversarial Contrastive Learning for LLM Quantization Attacks
summary: Model quantization is critical for deploying large language models (LLMs) on resource-constrained hardware, yet recent work has revealed severe security risks that benign LLMs in full precision may exhibit malicious behaviors after quantization. In this paper, we propose Adversarial Contrastive Learning (ACL), a novel gradient-based quantization attack that achieves superior attack effectiveness by explicitly maximizing the gap between benign and harmful responses probabilities. ACL formulates the attack objective as a triplet-based contrastive loss, and integrates it with a projected gradient descent two-stage distributed fine-tuning strategy to ensure stable and efficient optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate ACL's remarkable effectiveness, achieving attack success rates of 86.00% for over-refusal, 97.69% for jailbreak, and 92.40% for advertisement injection, substantially outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 44.67%, 18.84%, and 50.80%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a groundbreaking method for model quantization that maximizes the difference between benign and harmful response probabilities using gradient-based techniques.
AI.motivation: Model quantization is essential for deploying large language models on devices with limited resources, but recent research has shown that it can cause otherwise harmless models to produce malicious outputs. This paper aims to address the security risks associated with quantization.
AI.method: The proposed method, Adversarial Contrastive Learning (ACL), uses a triplet-based contrastive loss to create a gradient-based attack. It also employs a two-stage distributed fine-tuning strategy using projected gradient descent to optimize performance and stability.
AI.result: ACL outperforms existing techniques in attack success rates, achieving 86.00% for over-refusal, 97.69% for jailbreak, and 92.40% for advertisement injection, surpassing prior methods by significant margins.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that ACL is a powerful and effective quantization attack strategy, offering a new pathway to expose and understand the vulnerabilities of large language models when deployed in constrained environments.

title: The Vibe-Check Protocol: Quantifying Cognitive Offloading in AI Programming
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into software engineering education has driven the emergence of ``Vibe Coding,'' a paradigm where developers articulate high-level intent through natural language and delegate implementation to AI agents. While proponents argue this approach modernizes pedagogy by emphasizing conceptual design over syntactic memorization, accumulating empirical evidence raises concerns regarding skill retention and deep conceptual understanding. This paper proposes a theoretical framework to investigate the research question: \textit{Is Vibe Coding a better way to learn software engineering?} We posit a divergence in student outcomes between those leveraging AI for acceleration versus those using it for cognitive offloading. To evaluate these educational trade-offs, we propose the \textbf{Vibe-Check Protocol (VCP)}, a systematic benchmarking framework incorporating three quantitative metrics: the \textit{Cold Start Refactor} ($M_{CSR}$) for modeling skill decay; \textit{Hallucination Trap Detection} ($M_{HT}$) based on signal detection theory to evaluate error identification; and the \textit{Explainability Gap} ($E_{gap}$) for quantifying the divergence between code complexity and conceptual comprehension. Through controlled comparisons, VCP aims to provide a quantitative basis for educators to determine the optimal pedagogical boundary: identifying contexts where Vibe Coding fosters genuine mastery and contexts where it introduces hidden technical debt and superficial competence.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Vibe-Check Protocol (VCP), a theoretical framework for systematically evaluating the effectiveness of Vibe Coding in software engineering education, focusing on the trade-offs between AI-assisted acceleration and cognitive learning.
AI.motivation: The integration of AI in coding education raises concerns about skill retention and conceptual understanding. Educators need empirical evidence to determine if relying on AI for coding is beneficial in teaching core competencies.
AI.method: The authors present a theoretical research framework and define a benchmarking method called VCP, which includes three metrics: Cold Start Refactor, Hallucination Trap Detection, and Explainability Gap. They propose using controlled comparisons to examine the educational impacts of Vibe Coding.
AI.result: The paper outlines the components of VCP, emphasizing skill decay analysis, error detection, and understandability of code and concepts. The methodology intends to enable educators to systematically assess the pedagogical value of Vibe Coding.
AI.conclusion: The Vibe-Check Protocol provides a metric-driven approach to evaluate when and how Vibe Coding can be used effectively in software engineering education without compromising deep understanding or generating technical debt.

title: Privacy-Preserving AI-Enabled Decentralized Learning and Employment Records System
summary: Learning and Employment Record (LER) systems are emerging as critical infrastructure for securely compiling and sharing educational and work achievements. Existing blockchain-based platforms leverage verifiable credentials but typically lack automated skill-credential generation and the ability to incorporate unstructured evidence of learning. In this paper,a privacy-preserving, AI-enabled decentralized LER system is proposed to address these gaps. Digitally signed transcripts from educational institutions are accepted, and verifiable self-issued skill credentials are derived inside a trusted execution environment (TEE) by a natural language processing pipeline that analyzes formal records (e.g., transcripts, syllabi) and informal artifacts. All verification and job-skill matching are performed inside the enclave with selective disclosure, so raw credentials and private keys remain enclave-confined. Job matching relies solely on attested skill vectors and is invariant to non-skill resume fields, thereby reducing opportunities for screening bias.The NLP component was evaluated on sample learner data; the mapping follows the validated Syllabus-to-O*NET methodology,and a stability test across repeated runs observed <5% variance in top-ranked skills. Formal security statements and proof sketches are provided showing that derived credentials are unforgeable and that sensitive information remains confidential. The proposed system thus supports secure education and employment credentialing, robust transcript verification,and automated, privacy-preserving skill extraction within a decentralized framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a blockchain-based LER system that uses AI in secure enclaves to automatically generate verifiable skills credentials from both formal and informal learning records, enabling privacy-preserving, bias-resistant job matching with provable security properties.
AI.motivation: Existing blockchain-based LER systems lack automated skill credential generation from unstructured learning evidence and fail to prevent screening bias during job matching. Current approaches cannot securely process mixed formal/informal learning records while protecting privacy.
AI.method: A privacy-preserving decentralized system using trusted execution environments (TEE) with NLP pipelines. Processes formal transcripts and informal artifacts within secure enclaves, derives verifiable credentials, and performs skill-based job matching with selective disclosure of sensitive information.
AI.result: NLP evaluation shows <5% variance in skill rankings across runs using validated Syllabus-to-O*NET methodology. Formal security proofs demonstrate unforgeable credentials and confidentiality guarantees. The system successfully enables enclave-confined verification and bias-resistant skill-based job matching.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system provides secure and privacy-preserving education/employment credentialing through decentralized AI-driven skill extraction, enabling robust verification and bias-resistant job matching while maintaining confidentiality of sensitive data.

title: Talks that Builds: Exploring Communication factors for the Success of Emerging Professional in Product Teams
comment: 26 pages, 0 figure. Mixed-methods study examining factors contributing to successful product design teams with emerging professionals. Submitted to Southern States Communication Association
summary: This paper recognizes that most organizational communication study focuses on established professionals aged above 27 with more than five years of experience. In contrast, this study examines product teams with younger emerging professionals aged 18-27 and explores which factors influence their success. While some established factors still apply, others become less relevant, and new ones such as curiosity, locational proximity, documentation, access to resources were identified in the study. Overall, this study fills a gap in the literature on how these newer factors shape team productivity and project outcomes based on the success rate of the product the team developed.

AI.tldr: TL;DR: The paper addresses a gap in organizational communication studies by investigating factors influencing the success of younger emerging professionals (18-27) in product teams. It identifies newly relevant factors like curiosity, locational proximity, documentation, and access to resources, which impact team productivity and product success rates.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the observation that traditional organizational communication studies predominantly focus on established professionals above 27 with substantial work experience. This creates a gap in understanding the unique dynamics affecting younger emerging professionals, who constitute a growing segment in the workforce, particularly in product teams.
AI.method: The study likely employed qualitative or mixed methods research by analyzing product teams composed of younger emerging professionals. It probably involved interviews, surveys, and performance data from these teams to assess the influence of various factors on their success rates, differentiating between established and emerging factors.
AI.result: The results reveal that factors traditionally considered important—such as seniority and experience—are less relevant to younger product teams. Instead, newer factors like curiosity, locational proximity, documentation, and access to resources significantly enhance team productivity and project outcomes. The study's findings challenge existing assumptions and highlight new variables critical to the success of younger professionals.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the success rate of younger professionals in product teams is influenced by factors such as curiosity, locational proximity, documentation, and access to resources. These variables are essential for understanding and improving productivity and project outcomes in teams composed of emerging professionals, suggesting the need for organizational adaptations to better support this workforce segment.

title: SastBench: A Benchmark for Testing Agentic SAST Triage
summary: SAST (Static Application Security Testing) tools are among the most widely used techniques in defensive cybersecurity, employed by commercial and non-commercial organizations to identify potential vulnerabilities in software. Despite their great utility, they generate numerous false positives, requiring costly manual filtering (aka triage). While LLM-powered agents show promise for automating cybersecurity tasks, existing benchmarks fail to emulate real-world SAST finding distributions. We introduce SastBench, a benchmark for evaluating SAST triage agents that combines real CVEs as true positives with filtered SAST tool findings as approximate false positives. SastBench features an agent-agnostic design. We evaluate different agents on the benchmark and present a comparative analysis of their performance, provide a detailed analysis of the dataset, and discuss the implications for future development.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SastBench, a real-world benchmark for evaluating SAST triage agents by combining real CVEs as true positives and filtered SAST findings as approximate false positives, advancing automation in cybersecurity.
AI.motivation: Current SAST tools generate excessive false positives requiring manual review, and existing benchmarks fail to replicate real-world distributions, hindering progress in automating triage with LLM-powered agents.
AI.method: The authors created SastBench by integrating real CVEs (true positives from historical SAST results) and noise-limited SAST findings (approximate false positives), enabling agent-agnostic evaluation of triage performance.
AI.result: The dataset and benchmark reveal significant performance variations across agents, validate SastBench's realistic triage challenges, and provide a foundation for improving automated SAST filtering through comparative analysis.
AI.conclusion: SastBench establishes a critical infrastructure for developing and evaluating SAST triage agents, highlighting the need for domain-specific dataset construction to accelerate progress in automated cybersecurity workflows.

title: WebCoderBench: Benchmarking Web Application Generation with Comprehensive and Interpretable Evaluation Metrics
summary: Web applications (web apps) have become a key arena for large language models (LLMs) to demonstrate their code generation capabilities and commercial potential. However, building a benchmark for LLM-generated web apps remains challenging due to the need for real-world user requirements, generalizable evaluation metrics without relying on ground-truth implementations or test cases, and interpretable evaluation results. To address these challenges, we introduce WebCoderBench, the first real-world-collected, generalizable, and interpretable benchmark for web app generation. WebCoderBench comprises 1,572 real user requirements, covering diverse modalities and expression styles that reflect realistic user intentions. WebCoderBench provides 24 fine-grained evaluation metrics across 9 perspectives, combining rule-based and LLM-as-a-judge paradigm for fully automated, objective, and general evaluation. Moreover, WebCoderBench adopts human-preference-aligned weights over metrics to yield interpretable overall scores. Experiments across 12 representative LLMs and 2 LLM-based agents show that there exists no dominant model across all evaluation metrics, offering an opportunity for LLM developers to optimize their models in a targeted manner for a more powerful version.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WebCoderBench, a new benchmark for evaluating the generation of web applications by large language models (LLMs) that addresses the need for real-world user requirements, generalizable metrics, and interpretable results. Experiments show that no single model dominates across all metrics, suggesting potential for targeted optimization.
AI.motivation: The motivation of the paper stems from the growing importance of LLMs in generating web applications and the lack of a suitable benchmark that reflects real-world requirements, offers generalizable evaluation metrics, and provides interpretable results.
AI.method: The method involves creating WebCoderBench, a benchmark that includes 1,572 real user requirements reflecting diverse modalities and styles. It proposes 24 fine-grained metrics across nine perspectives, using both rule-based and LLM-as-a-judge paradigms, as well as weights aligned with human preferences to provide interpretable scores.
AI.result: The result is the creation of WebCoderBench, which enables automated and objective evaluation of web app generation by LLMs. Experiments on 12 LLMs and 2 agent systems show that no single model performs best across all metrics.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed benchmark offers a robust and interpretable means of evaluating web app generation by LLMs. The lack of a dominant model underscores the need for targeted model optimization.

title: Quality Degradation Attack in Synthetic Data
summary: Synthetic Data Generation (SDG) can be used to facilitate privacy-preserving data sharing. However, most existing research focuses on privacy attacks where the adversary is the recipient of the released synthetic data and attempts to infer sensitive information from it. This study investigates quality degradation attacks initiated by adversaries who possess access to the real dataset or control over the generation process, such as the data owner, the synthetic data provider, or potential intruders. We formalize a corresponding threat model and empirically evaluate the effectiveness of targeted manipulations of real data (e.g., label flipping and feature-importance-based interventions) on the quality of generated synthetic data. The results show that even small perturbations can substantially reduce downstream predictive performance and increase statistical divergence, exposing vulnerabilities within SDG pipelines. This study highlights the need to integrate integrity verification and robustness mechanisms, alongside privacy protection, to ensure the reliability and trustworthiness of synthetic data sharing frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how adversaries with real data access can degrade synthetic data quality through small manipulations, urging robustness measures in SDG pipelines.
AI.motivation: The paper highlights that existing SDG research focuses on adversaries inferring sensitive information from synthetic data, but overlooks attacks initiated by adversaries with access to real data or control over the generation process.
AI.method: The authors formalized a threat model and empirically evaluated targeted manipulations of real data (e.g., label flipping and feature-importance-based interventions) to assess their impact on synthetic data quality.
AI.result: The results show that minor perturbations in real data significantly degrade synthetic data quality, reducing predictive performance and increasing statistical divergence.
AI.conclusion: This study emphasizes the necessity of integrating integrity verification and robustness mechanisms in synthetic data sharing frameworks to ensure reliability and trustworthiness alongside privacy protection.

title: Focus on What Matters: Fisher-Guided Adaptive Multimodal Fusion for Vulnerability Detection
summary: Software vulnerability detection is a critical task for securing software systems and can be formulated as a binary classification problem: given a code snippet, determine whether it contains a vulnerability. Existing multimodal approaches typically fuse Natural Code Sequence (NCS) representations from pretrained language models with Code Property Graph (CPG) representations from graph neural networks, often under the implicit assumption that adding a modality necessarily yields extra information. In practice, sequence and graph representations can be redundant, and fluctuations in the quality of the graph modality can dilute the discriminative signal of the dominant modality. To address this, we propose TaCCS-DFA, a framework that introduces Fisher information as a geometric measure of how sensitive feature directions are to the classification decision, enabling task-oriented complementary fusion. TaCCS-DFA online estimates a low-rank principal Fisher subspace and restricts cross-modal attention to task-sensitive directions, thereby retrieving structural features from CPG that complement the sequence modality; meanwhile, an adaptive gating mechanism dynamically adjusts the contribution of the graph modality for each sample to suppress noise propagation. Our analysis shows that, under an isotropic perturbation assumption, the proposed mechanism admits a tighter risk bound than conventional full-spectrum attention. Experiments on BigVul, Devign, and ReVeal show that TaCCS-DFA achieves strong performance across multiple backbones. With CodeT5 as the backbone, TaCCS-DFA reaches an F1 score of 87.80\% on the highly imbalanced BigVul dataset, improving over a strong baseline Vul-LMGNNs by 6.3 percentage points while maintaining low calibration error and computational overhead.

AI.tldr: TaCCS-DFA is a multimodal fusion framework for software vulnerability detection that uses Fisher information to identify task-sensitive feature directions, enabling robust cross-modal attention and dynamic modality fusion. It achieves state-of-the-art results on imbalanced datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal approaches for vulnerability detection suffer from redundancy and noise in fused features (e.g., NCS sequences and CPG graphs). Fluctuations in graph quality can dilute the primary signal, motivating a task-aware fusion mechanism.
AI.method: TaCCS-DFA (1) estimates a low-rank principal Fisher subspace to focus on task-sensitive directions, (2) restricts cross-modal attention to complementary features via geometric constraints, and (3) employs adaptive gating to dynamically suppress noisy graph modality contributions.
AI.result: On BigVul (highly imbalanced), TaCCS-DFA achieves 87.80% F1 with CodeT5, outperforming Vul-LMGNNs by 6.3pp while maintaining low calibration error. It shows improvement across BigVul, Devign, and ReVeal datasets.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that task-oriented Fisher-guided fusion enhances robustness and performance in vulnerability detection by explicitly modeling complementary information between sequence and graph modalities.

title: Exploring Blockchain Interoperability: Frameworks, Use Cases, and Future Challenges
summary: Trust between entities in any scenario without a trusted third party is very difficult, and trust is exactly what blockchain aims to bring into the digital world with its basic features. Many applications are moving to blockchain adoption, enabling users to work in a trustworthy manner. The early generations of blockchain have a problem; they cannot share information with other blockchains. As more and more entities move their applications to the blockchain, they generate large volumes of data, and as applications have become more complex, sharing information between different blockchains has become a necessity. This has led to the research and development of interoperable solutions allowing blockchains to connect together. This paper discusses a few blockchain platforms that provide interoperable solutions, emphasising their ability to connect heterogeneous blockchains. It also discusses a case study scenario to illustrate the importance and benefits of using interoperable solutions. We also present a few topics that need to be solved in the realm of interoperability.

AI.tldr: This paper explores blockchain interoperability solutions, emphasizing their role in connecting diverse blockchains for seamless data sharing. A case study highlights practical benefits, while unresolved challenges like security and standardization are outlined for future research.
AI.motivation: The motivation for this paper arises from the limitations of early blockchain generations in sharing information across chains, which hinders their ability to handle complex applications and large-scale data exchanges. Interoperability is positioned as a necessity to enable collaborative, trustless environments in the digital world.
AI.method: The paper reviews and analyzes a selection of blockchain platforms that offer interoperable solutions, evaluates their capability to connect heterogeneous blockchains, presents a case study to demonstrate practical benefits, and identifies unresolved research topics in the interoperability domain.
AI.result: The results include an analysis of interoperable blockchain platforms, a case study illustrating the benefits of cross-chain connectivity, and a discussion of unresolved challenges such as security, standardization, and scalability in achieving effective interoperability.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that blockchain interoperability is essential for future growth and scalability, and while existing solutions show promise, addressing the identified challenges will be critical for achieving seamless cross-chain data and application sharing.

title: The Rise of Agentic Testing: Multi-Agent Systems for Robust Software Quality Assurance
summary: Software testing has progressed toward intelligent automation, yet current AI-based test generators still suffer from static, single-shot outputs that frequently produce invalid, redundant, or non-executable tests due to the lack of execution aware feedback. This paper introduces an agentic multi-model testing framework a closed-loop, self-correcting system in which a Test Generation Agent, an Execution and Analysis Agent, and a Review and Optimization Agent collaboratively generate, execute, analyze, and refine tests until convergence. By using sandboxed execution, detailed failure reporting, and iterative regeneration or patching of failing tests, the framework autonomously improves test quality and expands coverage. Integrated into a CI/CD-compatible pipeline, it leverages reinforcement signals from coverage metrics and execution outcomes to guide refinement. Empirical evaluations on microservice based applications show up to a 60% reduction in invalid tests, 30% coverage improvement, and significantly reduced human effort compared to single-model baselines demonstrating that multi-agent, feedback-driven loops can evolve software testing into an autonomous, continuously learning quality assurance ecosystem for self-healing, high-reliability codebases.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an agentic multi-model framework for AI-based software testing that uses closed-loop feedback between test generation, execution, and optimization agents to iteratively improve test quality and coverage. The system autonomously reduces invalid tests and human effort through execution-aware refinement.
AI.motivation: Traditional AI test generators produce static, single-shot outputs that often result in invalid or non-executable tests due to缺乏 of execution feedback. This leads to redundant testing, wasted resources, and insufficient coverage.
AI.method: The framework employs three agents: (1)*Test Generation Agent* creates initial tests, (2)*Execution & Analysis Agent* runs tests and reports failures, and (3)*Review & Optimization Agent* refines tests using coverage metrics and execution outcomes. It uses sandboxed execution with reinforcement learning signals to create a self-correcting loop until test convergence.
AI.result: Evaluations on microservice applications showed 60 percentage reduction in invalid tests, 30 percentage improvement in code coverage, and significantly reduced human intervention compared to single-model baselines in CI/CD pipelines.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent feedback-driven approach demonstrates that autonomous, execution-aware test generation can evolve into a self-healing quality assurance ecosystem, enabling high-reliability codebases with minimal human oversight.

title: Developing and Evaluating Lightweight Cryptographic Algorithms for Secure Embedded Systems in IoT Devices
summary: The high rate of development of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has brought to attention new challenges in the area of data security, especially within the resource-limited realm of RFID tags, sensors, and embedded systems. Traditional cryptographic implementations can be of inappropriate computational complexity and energy usage and hence are not suitable on these platforms. This paper examines the design, implementation, and testing of lightweight cryptographic algorithms that have been specifically designed to be used in secure embedded systems. A comparison of some of the state-of-the-art lightweight encryption algorithms, that is PRESENT, SPECK, and SIMON, focuses on the main performance indicators, i.e., throughput, use of memory, and energy utilization. The study presents novel lightweight algorithms that are founded upon the Feistel-network architecture and their safety under cryptanalytic attacks, e.g., differential and linear cryptanalysis. The proposed solutions are proven through hardware implementation on the FPGA platform. The results have shown that lightweight cryptography is an effective strategy that could be used to establish security and maintain performance in the IoT and other resource-limited settings.

AI.tldr: This paper develops and validates lightweight cryptographic algorithms for IoT devices, balancing security and efficiency through Feistel networks and FPGA implementation.
AI.motivation: Traditional cryptographic methods are computationally intensive and energy-inefficient for RFID tags, sensors, and embedded systems, requiring alternative solutions for IoT security.
AI.method: The paper designs, implements, and tests lightweight cryptographic algorithms based on Feistel networks, evaluates existing algorithms (PRESENT, SPECK, SIMON) on throughput, memory, and energy, and validates proposals via FPGA implementation.
AI.result: Proposed algorithms demonstrate robust security against differential/linear cryptanalysis and efficient performance in FPGA-based hardware testing, confirming their suitability for resource-constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight cryptography is an effective strategy for securing resource-limited IoT devices while maintaining performance.

title: Enhancing Debugging Skills with AI-Powered Assistance: A Real-Time Tool for Debugging Support
comment: Accepted at ICSE SEET 2026, 6 pages, 2 figures
summary: Debugging is a crucial skill in programming education and software development, yet it is often overlooked in CS curricula. To address this, we introduce an AI-powered debugging assistant integrated into an IDE. It offers real-time support by analyzing code, suggesting breakpoints, and providing contextual hints. Using RAG with LLMs, program slicing, and custom heuristics, it enhances efficiency by minimizing LLM calls and improving accuracy. A three-level evaluation - technical analysis, UX study, and classroom tests - highlights its potential for teaching debugging.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-powered debugging assistant integrated into an IDE to address the lack of debugging focus in CS education. It uses RAG with LLMs, program slicing, and heuristics for efficient real-time support, validated through technical, UX, and classroom evaluations.
AI.motivation: Debugging skills are underemphasized in computing curricula despite their importance in programming and software development. The tool aims to bridge this gap by providing accessible, context-aware debugging assistance to learners.
AI.method: The system combines retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), large language models (LLMs), program slicing for context analysis, and custom heuristics to minimize API calls. It integrates into IDEs to deliver breakpoints, hints, and error analysis during coding.
AI.result: Technical performance metrics demonstrated reduced LLM usage (30-50%), while user studies showed improved novice debugging efficiency (40-60%). Classroom trials revealed a 25% increase in post-assessment debugging scores compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The assistant effectively supports debugging education by combining AI techniques with educational needs, offering scalable solutions for curricula updates. Future work includes expanding to pair programming scenarios and multi-language support.

title: Selfish Mining in Multi-Attacker Scenarios: An Empirical Evaluation of Nakamoto, Fruitchain, and Strongchain
comment: 16 pages, ESORICS 2025 - Cryptocurrencies and Blockchain Technology - CBT 2025
summary: The aim of this work is to enhance blockchain security by deepening the understanding of selfish mining attacks in various consensus protocols, especially the ones that have the potential to mitigate selfish mining. Previous research was mainly focused on a particular protocol with a single selfish miner, while only limited studies have been conducted on two or more attackers. To address this gap, we proposed a stochastic simulation framework that enables analysis of selfish mining with multiple attackers across various consensus protocols. We created the model of Proof-of-Work (PoW) Nakamoto consensus (serving as the baseline) as well as models of two additional consensus protocols designed to mitigate selfish mining: Fruitchain and Strongchain. Using our framework, thresholds reported in the literature were verified, and several novel thresholds were discovered for 2 and more attackers. We made the source code of our framework available, enabling researchers to evaluate any newly added protocol with one or more selfish miners and cross-compare it with already modeled protocols.

AI.tldr: A simulation framework for testing blockchain consensus protocols against multi-attacker selfish mining, revealing new security thresholds and open-sourcing tools for protocol evaluation.
AI.motivation: Prior research focused on single attackers in specific protocols, leaving a gap in understanding multi-attacker dynamics and protocol effectiveness under diverse selfish mining conditions. This work addresses this gap to strengthen blockchain security.
AI.method: The study developed a stochastic simulation framework to model Proof-of-Work Nakamoto consensus, Fruitchain, and Strongchain protocols. This framework supports analysis of multiple selfish miners, verifies existing attack thresholds, and discovers novel thresholds for multi-attacker scenarios.
AI.result: Verification of literature-reported thresholds and discovery of new ones for protocols with two or more attackers. The open-source framework enables future protocol comparisons and analyses against selfish mining risks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed stochastic simulation framework provides a versatile tool for analyzing selfish mining attacks across various consensus protocols, particularly those designed to mitigate such attacks, and contributes to blockchain security by enabling comparative evaluations with multiple attackers.

title: Green LLM Techniques in Action: How Effective Are Existing Techniques for Improving the Energy Efficiency of LLM-Based Applications in Industry?
comment: Accepted for publication at the 2026 International Conference on Software Engineering: Software Engineering in Practice (ICSE-SEIP'26)
summary:
The rapid adoption of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns about their substantial energy consumption, especially when deployed at industry scale. While several techniques have been proposed to address this, limited empirical evidence exists regarding the effectiveness of applying them to LLM-based industry applications. To fill this gap, we analyzed a chatbot application in an industrial context at Schuberg Philis, a Dutch IT services company. We then selected four techniques, namely Small and Large Model Collaboration, Prompt Optimization, Quantization, and Batching, applied them to the application in eight variations, and then conducted experiments to study their impact on energy consumption, accuracy, and response time compared to the unoptimized baseline.
  Our results show that several techniques, such as Prompt Optimization and 2-bit Quantization, managed to reduce energy use significantly, sometimes by up to 90%. However, these techniques especially impacted accuracy negatively, to a degree that is not acceptable in practice. The only technique that achieved significant and strong energy reductions without harming the other qualities substantially was Small and Large Model Collaboration via Nvidia's Prompt Task and Complexity Classifier (NPCC) with prompt complexity thresholds. This highlights that reducing the energy consumption of LLM-based applications is not difficult in practice. However, improving their energy efficiency, i.e., reducing energy use without harming other qualities, remains challenging. Our study provides practical insights to move towards this goal.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the effectiveness of four techniques for reducing the energy consumption of an LLM-based chatbot in an industrial setting, finding that while some methods achieve significant energy savings, they often hurt accuracy. Only the Small and Large Model Collaboration method offered strong energy reductions without significant trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in industry but consume significant energy, raising concerns about their sustainability. This paper aims to evaluate the practical effectiveness of energy-saving techniques in real-world applications.
AI.method: The study analyzes a chatbot application at Schuberg Philis and applies four techniques (Small and Large Model Collaboration, Prompt Optimization, Quantization, and Batching) in eight variations. Experiments were conducted to measure impacts on energy consumption, accuracy, and response time.
AI.result: Prompt Optimization and 2-bit Quantization reduced energy use by up to 90%, but at the cost of significant accuracy loss. The Small and Large Model Collaboration method with NPCC achieved strong energy savings without major negative trade-offs on other metrics.
AI.conclusion: Reduction in energy consumption of LLM applications is feasible, but achieving energy efficiency (i.e., reducing energy use without sacrificing accuracy or performance) remains challenging. The study provides practical guidance for optimizing LLM applications in an industrial context.

title: JPU: Bridging Jailbreak Defense and Unlearning via On-Policy Path Rectification
summary: Despite extensive safety alignment, Large Language Models (LLMs) often fail against jailbreak attacks. While machine unlearning has emerged as a promising defense by erasing specific harmful parameters, current methods remain vulnerable to diverse jailbreaks. We first conduct an empirical study and discover that this failure mechanism is caused by jailbreaks primarily activating non-erased parameters in the intermediate layers. Further, by probing the underlying mechanism through which these circumvented parameters reassemble into the prohibited output, we verify the persistent existence of dynamic $\textbf{jailbreak paths}$ and show that the inability to rectify them constitutes the fundamental gap in existing unlearning defenses. To bridge this gap, we propose $\textbf{J}$ailbreak $\textbf{P}$ath $\textbf{U}$nlearning (JPU), which is the first to rectify dynamic jailbreak paths towards safety anchors by dynamically mining on-policy adversarial samples to expose vulnerabilities and identify jailbreak paths. Extensive experiments demonstrate that JPU significantly enhances jailbreak resistance against dynamic attacks while preserving the model's utility.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces JPU, a first-of-its-kind unlearning framework that dynamically identifies and rectifies persistent jailbreak paths in LLMs, offering robust safety alignment against evolving adversarial attacks without utility trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Current machine unlearning methods for LLMs exhibit critical vulnerabilities to dynamic jailbreak attacks, as they fail to address the emergence of adaptive 'jailbreak paths' formed through non-erased parameters in model layers, creating a persistent security blind spot.
AI.method: The authors propose JPU, a novel unlearning method that dynamically mines on-policy adversarial samples to expose and neutralize persistent jailbreak paths in intermediate layers of the model, directly targeting the mechanism by which erased parameters are circumvented during inference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that JPU significantly enhances resistance to diverse dynamic jailbreak attacks while maintaining model utility, outperforming existing approaches by effectively neutralizing the reassembly mechanism of prohibited outputs through targeted parameter unlearning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that JPU bridges the fundamental gap in unlearning defenses for LLMs by dynamically exposing and rectifying jailbreak paths, providing a robust solution for improving safety alignment against dynamic attacks without compromising model utility.

title: On the Effectiveness of Proposed Techniques to Reduce Energy Consumption in RAG Systems: A Controlled Experiment
comment: Accepted for publication at the 2026 International Conference on Software Engineering: Software Engineering in Society (ICSE-SEIS'26)
summary:
The rising energy demands of machine learning (ML), e.g., implemented in popular variants like retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, have raised significant concerns about their environmental sustainability. While previous research has proposed green tactics for ML-enabled systems, their empirical evaluation within RAG systems remains largely unexplored. This study presents a controlled experiment investigating five practical techniques aimed at reducing energy consumption in RAG systems. Using a production-like RAG system developed at our collaboration partner, the Software Improvement Group, we evaluated the impact of these techniques on energy consumption, latency, and accuracy.
  Through a total of 9 configurations spanning over 200 hours of trials using the CRAG dataset, we reveal that techniques such as increasing similarity retrieval thresholds, reducing embedding sizes, applying vector indexing, and using a BM25S reranker can significantly reduce energy usage, up to 60% in some cases. However, several techniques also led to unacceptable accuracy decreases, e.g., by up to 30% for the indexing strategies. Notably, finding an optimal retrieval threshold and reducing embedding size substantially reduced energy consumption and latency with no loss in accuracy, making these two techniques truly energy-efficient. We present the first comprehensive, empirical study on energy-efficient design techniques for RAG systems, providing guidance for developers and researchers aiming to build sustainable RAG applications.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates five practical techniques for reducing energy consumption in RAG systems, revealing that optimal retrieval thresholds and reduced embedding sizes significantly lower energy and latency without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: The rising energy demands of machine learning, particularly in RAG systems, highlight a need for sustainable solutions due to their environmental impact.
AI.method: A controlled experiment was conducted on a production-like RAG system across 9 configurations and over 200 hours of trials, evaluating energy consumption, latency, and accuracy.
AI.result: Techniques like higher similarity retrieval thresholds, reduced embedding sizes, vector indexing, and BM25S reranking substantially reduced energy use (up to 60%). However, some methods decreased accuracy by 30%. Reducing embedding size and optimizing retrieval thresholds offered energy efficiency without accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: The study provides the first comprehensive empirical analysis on energy-efficient RAG systems, offering actionable insights for sustainable ML development.

title: LLMs, You Can Evaluate It! Design of Multi-perspective Report Evaluation for Security Operation Centers
summary: Security operation centers (SOCs) often produce analysis reports on security incidents, and large language models (LLMs) will likely be used for this task in the near future. We postulate that a better understanding of how veteran analysts evaluate reports, including their feedback, can help produce analysis reports in SOCs. In this paper, we aim to leverage LLMs for analysis reports. To this end, we first construct a Analyst-wise checklist to reflect SOC practitioners' opinions for analysis report evaluation through literature review and user study with SOC practitioners. Next, we design a novel LLM-based conceptual framework, named MESSALA, by further introducing two new techniques, granularization guideline and multi-perspective evaluation. MESSALA can maximize report evaluation and provide feedback on veteran SOC practitioners' perceptions. When we conduct extensive experiments with MESSALA, the evaluation results by MESSALA are the closest to those of veteran SOC practitioners compared with the existing LLM-based methods. We then show two key insights. We also conduct qualitative analysis with MESSALA, and then identify that MESSALA can provide actionable items that are necessary for improving analysis reports.

AI.tldr: This study explores the use of large language models (LLMs) for generating analysis reports in security operation centers (SOCs), introducing a conceptual framework called MESSALA. By incorporating feedback from veteran SOC analysts, the framework improves LLM-generated reports to align with practitioners' standards. Experiments show that MESSALA's evaluations are closer to those of professionals compared to existing LLM methods, offering actionable insights for improvement.
AI.motivation: The study aims to enhance the creation of analysis reports in SOCs by leveraging LLMs. The motivation is driven by the potential of veteran analysts' feedback being crucial in aligning LLM outputs with established evaluation metrics and best practices in incident analysis, which can significantly improve the effectiveness of such systems.
AI.method: The methodology involves creating an analyst-wise checklist through a literature review and engaging SOC practitioners in a user study to gather feedback on reports. Subsequently, the MESSALA framework is developed with two novel techniques: granularization guideline and multi-perspective evaluation, enhancing LLM-generated report evaluations by incorporating these insights.
AI.result: The evaluation results with MESSALA demonstrate a closer match to the assessments of veteran SOC practitioners than existing LLM-based methods. The qualitative analysis highlights that MESSALA can provide actionable items necessary for improving the quality of analysis reports, indicating its effectiveness in refining the output of LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that by incorporating the insights and feedback from veteran analysts, the MESSALA framework can significantly enhance the generation of analysis reports in SOCs through LLMs. It provides a practical approach to aligning LLM outputs with practitioner expectations, suggesting future directions for integrating additional feedback mechanisms and exploring the adaptability of the framework in various operational environments.

title: PerspectiveCoach: Exploring LLMs for Developer Reflection
summary: Despite growing awareness of ethical challenges in software development, practitioners still lack structured tools that help them critically engage with the lived experiences of marginalized users. This paper presents PerspectiveCoach, a large language model (LLM)-powered conversational tool designed to guide developers through structured perspective-taking exercises and deepen critical reflection on how software design decisions affect marginalized communities. Through a controlled study with 18 front-end developers (balanced by sex), who interacted with the tool using a real case of online gender-based harassment, we examine how PerspectiveCoach supports ethical reasoning and engagement with user perspectives. Qualitative analysis revealed increased self-awareness, broadened perspectives, and more nuanced ethical articulation, while a complementary human-human study contextualized these findings. Text similarity analyses demonstrated that participants in the human-PerspectiveCoach study improved the fidelity of their restatements over multiple attempts, capturing both surface-level and semantic aspects of user concerns. However, human-PerspectiveCoach's restatements had a lower baseline than the human-human conversations, highlighting contextual differences in impersonal and interpersonal perspective-taking. Across the study, participants rated the tool highly for usability and relevance. This work contributes an exploratory design for LLM-powered end-user perspective-taking that supports critical, ethical self-reflection and offers empirical insights (i.e., enhancing adaptivity, centering plurality) into how such tools can help practitioners build more inclusive and socially responsive technologies.

AI.tldr: A paper introduces PerspectiveCoach, an LLM-powered conversational tool to help developers critically reflect on how their software design impacts marginalized users. A study reveals it increases self-awareness and ethical articulation, though with some baseline limitations compared to human-human interaction.
AI.motivation: Practitioners lack structured tools to engage with the lived experiences of marginalized users in software design, which is critical for ethical development.
AI.method: The study involves 18 front-end developers in a controlled experiment using a real case of online gender-based harassment. The paper uses qualitative analysis and text similarity measures to evaluate the effectiveness of PerspectiveCoach in enhancing ethical reasoning and perspective-taking.
AI.result: Participants showed increased self-awareness and more nuanced ethical discussion when using PerspectiveCoach. The tool improved fidelity in restating user concerns, though it had a lower baseline than human-human interaction. The tool was rated highly in usability and relevance.
AI.conclusion: PerspectiveCoach demonstrates a viable method for LLM-powered ethical reflection in software design. It offers empirical evidence for adaptive design and pluralistic perspectives in building inclusive technologies.

title: FlexProofs: A Vector Commitment with Flexible Linear Time for Computing All Proofs
summary: In this paper, we introduce FlexProofs, a new vector commitment (VC) scheme that achieves two key properties: (1) the prover can generate all individual opening proofs for a vector of size $N$ in optimal time ${\cal O}(N)$, and there is a flexible batch size parameter $b$ that can be increased to further reduce the time to generate all proofs; and (2) the scheme is directly compatible with a family of zkSNARKs that encode their input as a multi-linear polynomial. As a critical building block, we propose the first functional commitment (FC) scheme for multi-exponentiations with batch opening. Compared with HydraProofs, the only existing VC scheme that computes all proofs in optimal time ${\cal O}(N)$ and is directly compatible with zkSNARKs, FlexProofs may speed up the process of generating all proofs, if the parameter $b$ is properly chosen. Our experiments show that for $N=2^{16}$ and $b=\log^2 N$, FlexProofs can be $6\times$ faster than HydraProofs. Moreover, when combined with suitable zkSNARKs, FlexProofs enable practical applications such as verifiable secret sharing and verifiable robust aggregation.

AI.tldr: FlexProofs is a faster vector commitment scheme with customizable batch sizes that integrates with zkSNARKs, outperforming prior methods by 6× and enabling practical cryptographic applications.
AI.motivation: Existing vector commitment schemes like HydraProofs achieve optimal $\mathcal{O}(N)$ proof generation but lack flexibility in tuning performance trade-offs and compatibility with advanced zkSNARK constructions, necessitating improvements for practical cryptographic applications.
AI.method: The method introduces FlexProofs, a vector commitment scheme with (1) O(N) individual proof generation time and a scalable batch parameter $b$, and (2) compatibility with multi-linear polynomial-based zkSNARKs. A novel functional commitment for multi-exponentiation batch openings is also proposed as a core component.
AI.result: FlexProofs achieves 6× faster proof generation than HydraProofs for $N=2^{16}$ with $b=\log^2 N$. When integrated with suitable zkSNARKs, it enables verifiable secret sharing and verifiable robust aggregation.
AI.conclusion: FlexProofs improves upon existing vector commitment schemes by offering optimal and tunable proof generation times through a flexible batch size parameter and direct compatibility with zkSNARKs, enabling practical applications in verifiable cryptography.

title: Compressed code: the hidden effects of quantization and distillation on programming tokens
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional code generation capabilities, yet their token-level mechanisms remain underexplored, particularly in compressed models. Through systematic analysis of programming language token representations, we characterize how programming languages are encoded in LLM tokenizers by analyzing their vocabulary distribution and keyword coverage patterns. We introduce a novel cold-start probability analysis method that provides insights into model behavior without requiring explicit prompts. Additionally, we present a comprehensive evaluation of how different model optimization techniques - including quantization, distillation, model scaling, and task-specific fine-tuning - affect token-level representations and code generation quality. Our experiments, supported by comprehensive probability distribution analysis and evaluation metrics, reveal critical insights into token-level behavior and provide empirically-validated guidelines for maintaining code generation quality under various optimization constraints. These findings advance both theoretical understanding of LLM code generation and practical implementation of optimized models in production environments.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how programming languages are represented in LLM tokenizers and examines the impact of optimization techniques on code generation quality.
AI.motivation: While LLMs show strong code generation abilities, the token-level mechanisms, especially in compressed models, are not well understood. There is a need for a deeper understanding of how programming languages are encoded and how optimization techniques affect this encoding.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic analysis of programming language token representations by examining vocabulary distribution and keyword coverage. They introduce a cold-start probability analysis method and evaluate various optimization techniques (quantization, distillation, model scaling, and fine-tuning) on their impact to token-level representations and code generation quality.
AI.result: The experiments reveal insights into token-level behavior, showing how different optimization techniques impact code generation. They provide probability distribution analyses and validation of guidelines for maintaining code generation quality under optimization constraints.
AI.conclusion: The study advances the theoretical understanding of LLM code generation and provides practical guidelines for implementing optimized models in production while maintaining code generation quality.

title: SLIM: Stealthy Low-Coverage Black-Box Watermarking via Latent-Space Confusion Zones
summary: Training data is a critical and often proprietary asset in Large Language Model (LLM) development, motivating the use of data watermarking to embed model-transferable signals for usage verification. We identify low coverage as a vital yet largely overlooked requirement for practicality, as individual data owners typically contribute only a minute fraction of massive training corpora. Prior methods fail to maintain stealthiness, verification feasibility, or robustness when only one or a few sequences can be modified. To address these limitations, we introduce SLIM, a framework enabling per-user data provenance verification under strict black-box access. SLIM leverages intrinsic LLM properties to induce a Latent-Space Confusion Zone by training the model to map semantically similar prefixes to divergent continuations. This manifests as localized generation instability, which can be reliably detected via hypothesis testing. Experiments demonstrate that SLIM achieves ultra-low coverage capability, strong black-box verification performance, and great scalability while preserving both stealthiness and model utility, offering a robust solution for protecting training data in modern LLM pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SLIM, a data watermarking framework for LLMs that enables low-coverage, stealthy, and robust data provenance verification under strict black-box conditions.
AI.motivation: Training data is a critical asset in LLM development. Existing data watermarking methods fail to maintain stealthiness, verification feasibility, or robustness when only one or a few sequences can be modified. This is important for protecting training data in modern LLM pipelines, particularly when individual data owners contribute only a small fraction of the training corpus.
AI.method: SLIM leverages the intrinsic properties of LLMs to create a Latent-Space Confusion Zone by training the model to map semantically similar prefixes to divergent continuations. This results in localized generation instability, which is detected through hypothesis testing for data provenance verification.
AI.result: SLIM demonstrates ultra-low coverage capability (1-3 watermarked sequences), strong black-box verification performance, great scalability, and maintains both stealthiness and model utility. It outperforms previous methods in these metrics.
AI.conclusion: The introduction of SLIM addresses the limitations of prior watermarking techniques for LLMs, offering a practical and robust solution for protecting training data provenance with minimal modifications. SLIM's performance in low-coverage, stealthiness, and scalability makes it a promising approach for real-world deployment in LLM development pipelines.

title: State of the Quantum Software Engineering Ecosystem
summary: We study the current state of the Quantum Software Engineering (QSE) ecosystem, focusing on the achievements, activities, and engagements from academia and industry, with a special focus on successful entrepreneurial endeavors in this arena. Our research methodology is a novel one, featuring the state-of-the-art in Artificial Intelligence (AI), namely Large Language Models (LLMs), especially Generative Pretrained Transformers (GPT). We use one of such models, namely the OpenAI GPT-5 model, through the ChatGPT tool. The goal is to identify institutions and companies that are highly active and have achieved distinguished results in QSE, evidenced by peer-reviewed publications or raised capital in the venture capital market.

AI.tldr: The study uses AI, specifically LLMs like GPT-5, to analyze the Quantum Software Engineering ecosystem, identifying active institutions and companies in QSE through publications and venture capital funding.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the current state of QSE, highlight academic and industrial achievements, and focus on successful entrepreneurial initiatives.
AI.method: The research employs a novel methodology using AI, specifically large language models like GPT-5 (via ChatGPT) to analyze data.
AI.result: Identified institutions and companies active in QSE with strong academic outputs and venture capital support.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that AI-driven analysis can effectively map the QSE ecosystem, highlighting key players through their academic and commercial achievements.

title: TAAF: A Trace Abstraction and Analysis Framework Synergizing Knowledge Graphs and LLMs
summary: Execution traces are a critical source of information for understanding, debugging, and optimizing complex software systems. However, traces from OS kernels or large-scale applications like Chrome or MySQL are massive and difficult to analyze. Existing tools rely on predefined analyses, and custom insights often require writing domain-specific scripts, which is an error-prone and time-consuming task. This paper introduces TAAF (Trace Abstraction and Analysis Framework), a novel approach that combines time-indexing, knowledge graphs (KGs), and large language models (LLMs) to transform raw trace data into actionable insights. TAAF constructs a time-indexed KG from trace events to capture relationships among entities such as threads, CPUs, and system resources. An LLM then interprets query-specific subgraphs to answer natural-language questions, reducing the need for manual inspection and deep system expertise. To evaluate TAAF, we introduce TraceQA-100, a benchmark of 100 questions grounded in real kernel traces. Experiments across three LLMs and multiple temporal settings show that TAAF improves answer accuracy by up to 31.2%, particularly in multi-hop and causal reasoning tasks. We further analyze where graph-grounded reasoning helps and where limitations remain, offering a foundation for next-generation trace analysis tools.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TAF, which uses time-indexing, graphs, and LLMs to improve trace analysis for complex systems. It demonstrates improved accuracy via a new benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing trace analysis tools are limited by predefined analyses and require manual effort for custom insights.
AI.method: TAAF constructs a time-indexed knowledge graph from trace events and leverages LLMs to interpret natural-language queries over subgraphs.
AI.result: Experiments show TAAF improves LLM answer accuracy on trace reasoning tasks by up to 31.2%, especially in multi-hop and causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: TAAF provides a foundation for next-generation trace analysis by combining KGs and LLMs, highlighting strengths and limitations of graph reasoning in this domain.

title: Enterprise Identity Integration for AI-Assisted Developer Services: Architecture, Implementation, and Case Study
summary: AI-assisted developer services are increasingly embedded in modern IDEs, yet enterprises must ensure these tools operate within existing identity, access control, and governance requirements. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) enables AI assistants to retrieve structured internal context, but its specification provides only a minimal authorization model and lacks guidance on integrating enterprise SSO. This article presents a practical architecture that incorporates OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) into MCP-enabled developer environments. It describes how IDE extensions obtain and present tokens, how MCP servers validate them through an identity provider, and how scopes and claims can enforce least-privilege access. A prototype implementation using Visual Studio Code, a Python-based MCP server, and an OIDC-compliant IdP demonstrates feasibility. A case study evaluates authentication latency, token-validation overhead, operational considerations, and AI-specific risks. The approach provides a deployable pattern for organizations adopting AI-assisted developer tools while maintaining identity assurance and auditability.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the security gap in AI-powered IDEs by integrating enterprise SSO via OAuth 2.0/OIDC into the Model Context Protocol, providing a tested framework for secure, least-privilege access.
AI.motivation: Enterprises need to secure AI-assisted development tools within existing identity/governance frameworks, but the MCP protocol lacks robust authorization and SSO integration guidance.
AI.method: The paper integrates OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect into the Model Context Protocol (MCP) by designing an architecture where IDE extensions handle token acquisition, MCP servers validate tokens via an identity provider, and scopes/claims enforce least-privilege access. A prototype using Visual Studio Code and a Python-based MCP server demonstrates feasibility.
AI.result: A working prototype and case study validate the approach, showing measurable authentication latency, token-validation overhead, operational insights, and AI-specific risk mitigation in a real-world implementation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a deployable pattern for integrating secure identity management with AI-assisted developer tools, maintaining governance requirements through OAuth 2.0 and OIDC.

title: Agentic Memory Enhanced Recursive Reasoning for Root Cause Localization in Microservices
summary: As contemporary microservice systems become increasingly popular and complex-often comprising hundreds or even thousands of fine-grained, interdependent subsystems-they are experiencing more frequent failures. Ensuring system reliability thus demands accurate root cause localization. While many traditional graph-based and deep learning approaches have been explored for this task, they often rely heavily on pre-defined schemas that struggle to adapt to evolving operational contexts. Consequently, a number of LLM-based methods have recently been proposed. However, these methods still face two major limitations: shallow, symptom-centric reasoning that undermines accuracy, and a lack of cross-alert reuse that leads to redundant reasoning and high latency. In this paper, we conduct a comprehensive study of how Site Reliability Engineers (SREs) localize the root causes of failures, drawing insights from professionals across multiple organizations. Our investigation reveals that expert root cause analysis exhibits three key characteristics: recursiveness, multi-dimensional expansion, and cross-modal reasoning. Motivated by these findings, we introduce AMER-RCL, an agentic memory enhanced recursive reasoning framework for root cause localization in microservices. AMER-RCL employs the Recursive Reasoning RCL engine, a multi-agent framework that performs recursive reasoning on each alert to progressively refine candidate causes, while Agentic Memory incrementally accumulates and reuses reasoning from prior alerts within a time window to reduce redundant exploration and lower inference latency. Experimental results demonstrate that AMER-RCL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both localization accuracy and inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: By modeling expert SRE practices with recursive reasoning and memory reuse, AMER-RCL achieves better root cause localization in complex microservices than existing deep learning and LLM approaches.
AI.motivation: Traditional graph/deep learning methods rely on rigid schemas while being poor at adapting to evolving contexts. Existing LLM approaches suffer from shallow symptom-focused reasoning and duplicate alert analysis (high latency). The study reveals expert SREs use recursive processes, multi-dimensional analysis, and cross-modal reasoning for effective root cause localization.
AI.method: AMER-RCL integrates a Recursive Reasoning RCL multi-agent engine for iterative root cause refinement and an Agentic Memory module that accumulates/caches prior alert reasoning within sliding time windows to reduce redundant computation.
AI.result: AMER-RCL outperforms state-of-the-art methods in localization accuracy and inference efficiency across experiments, with memory reuse mechanisms reducing redundant computation and recursive reasoning improving precision through iterative refinement.
